title: Problem with assignments larger than 64bit
body:
---


Author Name: **Amir Gonnen**
Original Redmine Issue: 214 from https://www.veripool.org
Original Date: 2010-01-27
Original Assignee: Wilson Snyder (@wsnyder)

---

verilator-3.720, compiled with MinGW, and ran: verilator.exe -Wno-lint -Wno-UNOPTFLAT -language 1364-2001 --trace --compiler msvc --cc top.v
It took me some time to realize that there is some problem with the code emitted by Verilator.
consider the following:
```
wire [44:0]	line0;
wire [44:0]	line1;
wire [8:0]	b;
wire [89:0]	data_in;
...
assign {line0[4:0],line1[4:0]} = b[0] ? {data_in[4:0],data_in[49:45]} :  {data_in[49:45],data_in[4:0]};
assign {line0[9:5],line1[9:5]} = b[1] ? {data_in[9:5],data_in[54:50]} :  {data_in[54:50],data_in[9:5]};
assign {line0[14:10],line1[14:10]} = b[2] ? {data_in[14:10],data_in[59:55]} :  {data_in[59:55],data_in[14:10]};
assign {line0[19:15],line1[19:15]} = b[3] ? {data_in[19:15],data_in[64:60]} :  {data_in[64:60],data_in[19:15]};
assign {line0[24:20],line1[24:20]} = b[4] ? {data_in[24:20],data_in[69:65]} :  {data_in[69:65],data_in[24:20]};
assign {line0[29:25],line1[29:25]} = b[5] ? {data_in[29:25],data_in[74:70]} :  {data_in[74:70],data_in[29:25]};
assign {line0[34:30],line1[34:30]} = b[6] ? {data_in[34:30],data_in[79:75]} :  {data_in[79:75],data_in[34:30]};
assign {line0[39:35],line1[39:35]} = b[7] ? {data_in[39:35],data_in[84:80]} :  {data_in[84:80],data_in[39:35]};
assign {line0[44:40],line1[44:40]} = b[8] ? {data_in[44:40],data_in[89:85]} :  {data_in[89:85],data_in[44:40]};

```

Given b is 0, I expected line0 to be equal to the highest 45 bit of data_in.
However I found out that line0[39:32] is always 0, no matter what data_in was.
Trying to figure this out, I checked the emitted C code and found out that it looks something like this:
```
vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffe0) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffe0) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfffffc1f) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfffffc1f) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffff83ff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffff83ff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfff07fff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfff07fff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfe0fffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xfe0fffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xc1ffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xc1ffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0x3fffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0x3fffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line0) 
	   | ...some calculation...
 vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1 
	= ((VL_ULL(0xffffffff) & vlSymsp->TOP__v.line1) 
	   | ...some calculation...

```
The problem is that while line0 (and line1) are longer than 32bit, masking is still done with 32bit mask (@((VL_ULL(0xffffffff)@).
So the assignment of the last 5 bits first sets all bits above the 32'th bit to zero, which is wrong as it should set to zero only the highest 5 bits. I would expect the mask to be larger and include at least 45 bits.

Thanks,

Amir
